steps.title: Identify what the research question asks for
steps.content:
“An outstanding question is whether perceived category similarity predicts consumers' likelihood of purchasing brand extensions.” This explicit statement defines the two variables needed: perceived category similarity and likelihood of purchase.

The study already measured the likelihood (probability changes for each pair); the missing piece is perceived category similarity.

steps.title: Find the design that provides the missing variable
steps.content:
Choice A — Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of the product categories in each extended-brand pair, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair. asks households to rate category similarity for each pair, then correlates those ratings with the probability changes already calculated—directly testing whether perceived similarity predicts purchase behavior.

The other three pursue different variables (brand recognition in C and D; within-category similarity in B) or use flawed methodology, none of which would answer the research question.

That matches Choice A — Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of the product categories in each extended-brand pair, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair..
